reasoning: A is strongest: it names the actual decision-analytic traps (attribute substitution, escalation of commitment, false dilemma), runs an explicit outside-view base rate with a competing reference class, builds a premortem, and lands a calibrated verdict (65% confidence) tied to three named facts it would verify first. B covers the same terrain well with a clean case-for/case-against structure and a concrete counter-offer, but it states burn and runway figures more flatly and surfaces fewer named biases and less explicit uncertainty quantification. C reaches the correct verdict with a usable term sheet and correctly flags the single-customer red flag, but its reasoning is thinner: it asserts the runway math without much stress-testing, does little steelmanning of the take-it case, and names no cognitive traps. All three correctly identify that the runway-vs-frozen-roadmap trade is near break-even and that reusability is the hinge; A handles that denominator-and-opportunity-cost logic most rigorously.
